answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem *Visal* (Bang-e-Dara Part 2, ch. 27), the speaker celebrates achieving mystical union (*visal*) with the beloved—symbolized as the long-sought flower that the nightingale (the lover/poet) has finally attained. This union resolves prior torment, transforms the lover's inner darkness into a reflective mirror of the beloved's eternal light, and reveals true liberty through surrender.[^1]

Key passages include:

> The flower whose search, O Nightingale, would make me pine,  
> By blessed fate, that very flower is finally mine.[^2]

> The lustre of this love makes my dark dust a mirror bright,  
> And in it shines the image of my timeless friend's own light.[^3]

> By entering chains, I found my liberty,  
> My home was built upon the wreck of me.[^4]

> You cast a single glance, and taught surrender's art,  
> O blessed day, that set a fire to my worthless heart![^5]

The poem contrasts the lover's former restlessness and failure (e.g., "quicksilver" impatience, "blood-filled breast" with breaths like blades) against the peace and radiance of union.[^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 27 "Visal" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, [p-cade15](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-cade15).

[^3]: *Ibid.*, [p-3a1ddc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-3a1ddc).

[^4]: *Ibid.*, [p-456772](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-456772).

[^5]: *Ibid.*, [p-418f98](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-418f98).

[^6]: *Ibid.*, [p-136e94](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-136e94), [p-894b93](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-894b93).
